papergraph.title: A review and outlook on anionic and cationic redox in Ni-, Li- and Mn-rich layered oxides LiMeO2 (Me = Li, Ni, Co, Mn)
papergraph.citation: Bixian Ying et al. (2023) A review and outlook on anionic and cationic redox in Ni-, Li- and Mn-rich layered oxides LiMeO2 (Me = Li, Ni, Co, Mn) arXiv:2310.01295v2
papergraph.abstract: The present work reviews the charge compensation in Ni based layered oxides (LiNi1-xMexO2 with x <= 0.2, Me = Co, Mn, space group R-3m) relating performance parameters to changes in the electronic and crystallographic structure of the cathode materials. Upon charge and discharge two fundamentally different redox mechanisms are observed: At low and medium states of charge (SOCs) charge compensation takes mainly place at oxygen sites while electron density is shifted from the oxygen lattice to nickel (formation of sigma bonds). At high SOCs the shift of electron density from the transition metals to oxygen (formation of pi bonds) enables an additional redox process but also oxygen release from the transition metal host structure and subsequent detrimental reactions. Depending on the Ni:Co:Mn content, both processes lead to characteristic features in the voltage profile of the cathode materials and performance parameters like the capacity, the cycling stability and the open cell voltage become a function of the composition.
papergraph.scenario: Ni-, Li-, and Mn-rich layered oxide cathode materials
papergraph.benchmark: voltage profile | capacity | cycling stability | open cell voltage
papergraph.contribution: Systematic review linking anionic and cationic redox mechanisms to structural and electronic changes in layered oxide cathodes, and their impact on electrochemical performance.
papergraph.limitation: Depending on the Ni:Co:Mn content, both processes lead to characteristic features in the voltage profile of the cathode materials and performance parameters like the capacity, the cycling stability and the open cell voltage become a function of the composition.

papergraph.title: Cathode Materials for Lithium Ion Batteries (LIBs): A Review on Materials related aspects towards High Energy Density LIBs
papergraph.citation: Ambesh Dixit (2020) Cathode Materials for Lithium Ion Batteries (LIBs): A Review on Materials related aspects towards High Energy Density LIBs arXiv:2008.10896v2
papergraph.abstract: This article reviews the development of cathode materials for secondary lithium ion batteries since its inception with the introduction of lithium cobalt oxide in early 1980s. The time has passed and numerous cathode materials are designed and developed to realize not only the enhanced capacity but also the power density simultaneously. However, there are numerous challenges such as the cyclic stability of cathode materials, their structural and thermal stability, higher operating voltage together with high ionic and electronic conductivity for efficient ion and charge transport during charging and discharging. This article will cover the development of materials in chronological order classifying as the lithium ion cathode materials in different generations. The ternary oxides such as LiTMOx (TM=Transition Metal) are considered as the first generation materials, whereas modified ternary and quaternary oxide systems are considered as the second generation materials. The current i.e. third generation includes complex oxide systems with higher lithium content such as Li2TMSiO4 aiming for higher energy density. Further, developments are heading towards lithium metal based batteries with a possibility for very high energy densities.

papergraph.title: Voltage-dependent charge compensation mechanism and cathode electrolyte interface stability of the lithium-ion battery cathode materials LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2, LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 and LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 studied by photoelectron spectroscopy
papergraph.citation: Maximilian Mellin et al. (2025) Voltage-dependent charge compensation mechanism and cathode electrolyte interface stability of the lithium-ion battery cathode materials LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2, LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 and LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 studied by photoelectron spectroscopy doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-s4hrh
papergraph.abstract: The wide utilization of Ni-rich oxides as a cathode material in lithium-ion batteries (LIB) for high-energy applications remains a challenge due to their unstable behavior at high state-of-charge (SOC) increasing the risk of thermal runaway. To understand this instability, in-depth knowledge of the involved redox processes is needed, which depend on the electronic structure of these materials as well as their interaction with the electrolyte. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) is a promising method to gain information on the electronic structure while inherently limited to a low information depth. The latter is especially problematic for LIB materials, due to the formation of interphase layers on both the anode and the cathode. In our previous studies, we developed an in vacuo scratching method, which allowed the removal of a large fraction of the cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) and applied the method to the cathode materials LiCoO2 (LCO) and LiNi0.3Mn0.3Co0.3O2 (NMC333) to investigate the charge state dependent transition metal core spectra, exhibiting high spectral quality. In this study, the in vacuo scratching method was applied to the Ni-rich layered oxide compounds, including LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2 (NMC622), LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811) and 2 LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA). All three materials showed an equal, high oxidation state of Ni in the pristine state. The Ni2+/3+ and Ni3+/4+ redox couples were identified by the analysis of the Ni 2p core spectra as a function of voltage. Based on the Co 2p and O 1s photoelectron spectra, a shift to lower binding energies was observed due to the downward shift of the Fermi level. Charging the materials to high voltages up to 4.8 V vs. Li+/Li resulted in fully deintercalated compounds, as shown by the Li 1s photoelectron spectra, while no further change in the Ni 2p photoelectron spectra suggests deviations from the cationic redox process. The cathode-electrolyte interface (CEI) composition was also analyzed for non-scratched samples at several voltage steps in the typical operational range of 3.0 to 4.2 V vs. Li+/Li indicating a difference in the amount of semi-carbonates and lithium alkoxides. At high voltages of 4.5/ 4.8 V vs. Li+/Li, considerable changes were observed in the CEI due to the onset of decomposition reactions of the CEI components.
improvements: Abstract: The wide utilization of Ni-rich oxides as a cathode material in lithium-ion batteries (LIB) for high-energy applications remains a challenge due to their unstable behavior at high state-of-charge (SOC) increasing the risk of thermal runaway. To understand this instability, in-depth knowled...[truncated]
papergraph.method: X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) with in vacuo scratching
papergraph.benchmark: compensation mechanism and cathode electrolyte interface stability
papergraph.contribution: Identification of Ni2+/3+ and Ni3+/4+ redox couples via Ni 2p spectra as a function of voltage; observation of Fermi level shift via Co 2p and O 1s spectra; evidence of non-cationic redox at high voltage (4.8 V) via absence of Ni 2p spectral change despite full Li deintercalation; voltage-dependent CEI compositional evolution including semi-carbonates and lithium alkoxides
papergraph.limitation: XPS is inherently limited to a low information depth, especially problematic due to CEI layer formation on cathodes

papergraph.title: Electronic Structure Modeling of Electrochemical Reactions at Electrode/Electrolyte Interfaces in Lithium Ion Batteries
papergraph.citation: Kevin Leung (2013) Electronic Structure Modeling of Electrochemical Reactions at Electrode/Electrolyte Interfaces in Lithium Ion Batteries doi:10.1021/jp308929a
papergraph.abstract: We review recent ab initio molecular dynamics studies of electrode/electrolyte interfaces in lithium ion batteries. Our goals are to introduce experimentalists to simulation techniques applicable to models which are arguably most faithful to experimental conditions so far, and to emphasize to theorists that the inherently interdisciplinary nature of this subject requires bridging the gap between solid and liquid state perspectives. We consider liquid ethylene carbonate (EC) decomposition on lithium intercalated graphite, lithium metal, oxide-coated graphite, and spinel manganese oxide surfaces. These calculations are put in the context of more widely studied water-solid interfaces. Our main themes include kinetically controlled two-electron-induced reactions, the breaking of a previously much neglected chemical bond in EC, and electron tunneling. Future work on modeling batteries at atomic lengthscales requires capabilities beyond state-of-the-art, which emphasizes that applied battery research can and should drive fundamental science development.
improvements: Abstract: We review recent ab initio molecular dynamics studies of electrode/electrolyte interfaces in lithium ion batteries. Our goals are to introduce experimentalists to simulation techniques applicable to models which are arguably most faithful to experimental conditions so far, and to emphasize...[truncated]

papergraph.contribution: introduction of ab initio molecular dynamics as a simulation technique for modeling electrochemical reactions at electrode/electrolyte interfaces with high fidelity to experimental conditions; emphasis on bridging solid and liquid state perspectives; identification of kinetically controlled two-electron-induced reactions, EC bond breaking, and electron tunneling in interfacial processes

papergraph.title: Mechanistic Study of Functional-Solvent-Based Electrolytes for High Voltage Lithium Batteries
papergraph.citation: Md Farhan Hossain et al. (2025) Mechanistic Study of Functional-Solvent-Based Electrolytes for High Voltage Lithium Batteries doi:10.1149/ma2025-013298mtgabs
papergraph.abstract:
The pervasive use of Ni-rich cathode active materials, e.g., LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811), for high energy density Li-ion batteries (LIBs) has been hindered by the rapid capacity decay when cycled at high cutoff voltages, due to electrolyte decomposition of the conventional carbonate solvent-based electrolytes,1 parasitic oxidation reactions at the electrolyte/cathode interface,2 irreversible phase changes in the cathodes,3 and dissolution of transition metals into electrolytes.4 Therefore, R&D efforts are needed to tackle the above technical challenges of Ni-rich cathode-based LIBs before they become commercially viable.
 In our previous study,5 a ternary of fluorinated solvents was used to formulate a high voltage stable electrolyte (HVE) which was used in Ni-rich cathode based, high-voltage LIBs. These HVE-based batteries demonstrated a significant improvement in cycle stability when operated at a charge cutoff voltage of 4.5 V (vs Li/Li+). In this study, we investigated the roles of single-solvents and co-solvents in the cycle stability of the high-voltage lithium batteries. Several families of organic solvents, such as fluorinated carbonates (FC) and organosilicon (Si) based solvents, were investigated (Figure 1). We will show that some unique combinations of the functional solvents could lead to exceptionally stable cycle performance in the Ni-rich cathode-based LIBs. We will discuss possible fundamental mechanisms responsible for the observed performance stability of the high-voltage LIBs using ex-situ XPS, density functional theory calculations, and other characterization techniques.

papergraph.title: Enhanced Aluminium Passivation properties of lithium nonafluoro-tert-butoxide: Designing Electrolyte Additives for High-Voltage 4.8 V LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4-graphite batteries
papergraph.citation: Binayak Roy et al. (2024) Enhanced Aluminium Passivation properties of lithium nonafluoro-tert-butoxide: Designing Electrolyte Additives for High-Voltage 4.8 V LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4-graphite batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-tjmgp
papergraph.abstract: LiNi0.5Mn1.5O4 (LNMO) cathodes offer high energy density in Li-ion batteries due to their higher operational potential than conventional cathode materials. However, their practical application in Li-ion batteries is limited by the active metal (e.g., Mn) dissolution, which irreversibly compromises the graphite anode of a Li-ion battery. In this work, lithium nonafluoro-tert-butoxyborate (LiONFtb) has been tested as an additive in LNMO-graphite system. The electrochemical stability analysis of the LiONFtb salt on the aluminium current collector revealed a new passivation mechanism, without the formation of cracks and pits, which are otherwise commonly observed in the case of LiPF6-containing electrolytes. Adding 1 wt% LiONFtb to the commercial LiPF6-based electrolyte is demonstrated to improve the high voltage stability of the electrolyte in LNMO-Li half-cell. The LiONFtb additive was also found to promote better Li+ de-/intercalation behaviour in graphite-Li half-cell. The long-term cycling of the LNMO-graphite full cell with the 1% LiONFtb additive electrolyte demonstrated higher initial discharge capacity (121 mAh/g) and higher capacity retention (62%) than the LiPF6 containing electrolyte (105 mAh/g and 9.2%, respectively) after 100 cycles. The post-cycling analysis reveals that the LiONFtb additive helps to retain the morphology of the LNMO particles and reduces the Mn dissolution during cycling. The positive influence of the LiONFtb additive on reducing the cathode particle cracking and active metal dissolution makes it a viable electrolyte additive candidate for next-generation high-voltage Li-ion batteries.

papergraph.title: Electrolyte stability and instability in lithium-ion batteries
papergraph.citation: Jason S. Terreblanche et al. (2024) Electrolyte stability and instability in lithium-ion batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-nmnr8
papergraph.abstract: The development of stable electrolyte solutions is critical for improving the lifetime and performance of lithium-ion batteries. Electrolyte instability is a prominent issue with many next-generation electrode materials, such as Ni-rich cathodes, with recent reports identifying ethylene carbonate (EC) as a bad actor at the cathode surface. In this letter, electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, and post-mortem X-ray and solution NMR studies were combined to investigate electrolyte degradation phenomena in Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes. One key finding is that the mechanism for improved performance in EC-free electrolyte arises from improved stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces. Suppressed solvent oxidation reactions at the cathode, and hence less formation and cross over of acidic species, combined with a graphite SEI more resilient towards acid-mediated decomposition, are shown to be important to improve capacity retention. These insights are helpful to understand and mitigate degradation in cells with Ni-rich cathodes.

papergraph.title: Advanced High-Voltage Electrolyte Design Using Poly(ethylene oxide) and High-Concentration Ionic Liquids for All-Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Mingjie Zhang et al. (2024) Advanced High-Voltage Electrolyte Design Using Poly(ethylene oxide) and High-Concentration Ionic Liquids for All-Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-5sqth
papergraph.abstract: Poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO) based solid polymer electrolytes (SPEs) are one of the most promising materials for solid-state lithium metal batteries (LMBs) due to their inherent safety advantages, however, they suffer from insufficient room temperature ionic conductivity (up to 10−6 S cm−1) and limited oxidation stability (<4V). In this study, a novel “polymer-in-high concentrated ionic liquid” (PiHCIL) electrolyte composed of PEO, N-propyl-N-methylpyrrolidinium bis(fluorosulfonyl) imide (C3mpyrFSI) ionic liquid (IL) and LiFSI is designed. The EO: [Li/IL] ratio has been widely varied and their physical and electrochemical properties have been explored. The Li-coordination and solvation structure has been explored through FTIR spectroscopy and solid-state magic angle spinning (MAS) NMR. The designed electrolyte provides promisingly high oxidative stability of 5.1V and offers high ambient temperature ionic conductivity of 5.6 x10-4 S cm-1. Li|Li symmetric cell cycling showed very stable and reversible cycling of Li metal over 100 cycles and smooth, dendrite-free deposition morphology. All-solid-state cells using composite LFP cathode exhibited promising cycling with 99.2% capacity retention at C/5 rate over 100 cycles. Therefore, the novel approach of PiHCIL enables a new pathway to design high-performing SPEs for high-energy density all-solid-state LMBs.

papergraph.title: Research Progress on Halide Solid‐State Electrolytes: Synthesis, Mechanism, and Modification
papergraph.citation: W. Shen et al. (2026) Research Progress on Halide Solid‐State Electrolytes: Synthesis, Mechanism, and Modification doi:10.1002/cey2.70170
papergraph.abstract: Halide solid‐state electrolytes (HSSEs) have gained significant attention as key components for all‐solid‐state lithium ion batteries due to their notable advantages, including high ionic conductivity (> 1 mS cm−1), wide electrochemical window (> 4 V vs. Li/Li⁺), and good compatibility with high‐voltage cathodes. Despite progress, major challenges such as ionic conductivity, air stability, and interface compatibility still remain. This review systematically summarizes their representative classifications (e.g., Lia‐M‐X8, Lia‐M‐X6, Lia‐M‐X4, LiaMbOcXd, M = In, Y, Al…; X = Cl, F, Br…), synthesis methods (e.g., solid phase, liquid phase, gas phase), and ion conduction mechanisms (e.g., vacancy‐driven transport). The merits and demerits of different synthesis methods are analyzed, and the factors affecting ion conductivity are also discussed. Moreover, various modification strategies (e.g., structure optimization, doping, and surface coating) are analyzed to address the above issues. Meanwhile, research guidelines for developing advanced HSSEs are also proposed. Additionally, we provide a systematic outlook on HSSEs in terms of novel synthesis methods and interface modification technologies (such as plasma and supercritical fluid technologies), high‐precision characterization methods for interface components (such as solid‐state nuclear magnetic resonance), artificial intelligence (AI)‐assisted mechanism analysis, and material synthesis. This review offers new research insights into the design and development of advanced solid‐state electrolytes for energy storage.

papergraph.title: A free-standing sulfone-based solid-state electrolyte mitigating the release of crystal water from the Prussian blue cathode for high voltage potassium batteries
papergraph.citation: Seokbum Kang et al. (2024) A free-standing sulfone-based solid-state electrolyte mitigating the release of crystal water from the Prussian blue cathode for high voltage potassium batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-gt14s
papergraph.abstract: Prussian blue (PB) and its analogues are emerging cathode materials for potassium batteries due to their 3D open framework allowing facile ion diffusion. However, the crystal water, an inherent component of PB structure, can be released into the electrolytes especially during high voltage operation, causing detrimental side reactions and rapid degradation of potassium batteries. To address this challenge, we present a free-standing sulfone-based solid-state electrolyte (FSSE) comprising potassium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide, dimethyl sulfone, and UV-cured trimethylolpropane ethoxylate triacrylate network. The FSSE effectively prevents the release of crystal water from PB cathode while exhibiting suitable electrolyte properties including decent ionic conductivity (2.2 × 10−4 S cm−1 at 25 °C), exceptional oxidative stability (~4.8 V vs. K+/K), and intimate contact with porous composite PB electrodes. K||PB cells utilizing FSSE demonstrate superior cyclability compared to a liquid carbonate electrolyte (74.6% vs 53.3 % capacity retention over 200 cycles). The solid-state electrolyte possessing the ability to block crystal water provides a promising avenue for exploring diverse PB cathode materials for high-voltage potassium batteries.
strengths: decent ionic conductivity (2.2 × 10−4 S cm−1 at 25 °C)
exceptional oxidative stability (~4.8 V vs. K+/K)
intimate contact with porous composite PB electrodes
superior cyclability (74.6% capacity retention over 200 cycles)
improvements: mitigates release of crystal water from Prussian blue cathode

papergraph.title: Highly Antioxidative Lithium Salt Enables High-Voltage Ether Electrolyte for Lithium Metal Battery
papergraph.citation: Jian He et al. (2024) Highly Antioxidative Lithium Salt Enables High-Voltage Ether Electrolyte for Lithium Metal Battery arXiv:2409.18344v1
papergraph.abstract: Ether-based electrolytes exhibit excellent compatibility with Li metal anodes, but their instability at high voltages limits their use in high-voltage Li metal batteries. To address this issue, we introduce an alternative perfluorobutane sulfonate (LiPFBS) / dimethoxyethane (DME) electrolyte to stabilize DME in a 4.6 V Li-Li LCO battery. Our study focuses on the formation of solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) and cathode-electrolyte interphase (CEI) layers compared to the LiTFSI/DME electrolyte. We demonstrate that LiPFBS helps maintain DME's compatibility in SEI formation. Additionally, a durable CEI layer derived from PFBS$^{-}$ enhances the performance of the cell at high voltages by forming a robust, inorganic-dominant CEI layer. A PFBS$^{-}$-derived CEI significantly enhances the overall performance of the full cell under high voltage conditions.
papergraph.contribution: LiPFBS enables stable high-voltage operation of ether-based electrolyte via formation of robust, inorganic-dominant CEI layer
papergraph.limitation: A PFBS$^{-}$-derived CEI significantly enhances the overall performance of the full cell under high voltage conditions.

papergraph.title: Deciphering Interphase Instability of Lithium Metal Batteries with Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes at Elevated Temperatures
papergraph.citation: Tao Meng et al. (2024) Deciphering Interphase Instability of Lithium Metal Batteries with Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes at Elevated Temperatures arXiv:2401.05671v1
papergraph.abstract: Lithium metal batteries (LMBs), when coupled with a localized high-concentration electrolyte and a high-voltage nickel-rich cathode, offer a solution to the increasing demand for high energy density and long cycle life. However, the aggressive electrode chemistry poses safety risks to LMBs at higher temperatures and cutoff voltages. Here, we decipher the interphase instability in LHCE-based LMBs with a Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathode at elevated temperatures. Our findings reveal that the generation of fluorine radicals in the electrolyte induces the solvent decomposition and consequent chain reactions, thereby reconstructing the cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) and degrading battery cyclability. As further evidenced, introducing an acid scavenger of dimethoxydimethylsilane (DODSi) significantly boosts CEI stability with suppressed microcracking. A Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2||Li cell with this DODSi-functionalized LHCE achieves an unprecedented capacity retention of 93.0% after 100 cycles at 80 °C. This research provides insights into electrolyte engineering for practical LMBs with high safety under extreme temperatures.
papergraph.benchmark: capacity retention | CEI stability | microcracking suppression
papergraph.contribution: Identification of fluorine radical–driven solvent decomposition as the root cause of CEI reconstruction and cyclability degradation in LHCE-based LMBs at elevated temperatures; demonstration that DODSi acid scavenger suppresses microcracking and enables 93.0% capacity retention after 100 cycles at 80 °C.
papergraph.limitation: Here, we decipher the interphase instability in LHCE-based LMBs with a Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathode at elevated temperatures.

papergraph.title: Solid-in-Solid" Electrolyte via Scalable Melting Infiltration Method for High-Voltage Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries
papergraph.citation: Tongtai Ji et al. (2025) "Solid-in-Solid" Electrolyte via Scalable Melting Infiltration Method for High-Voltage Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-hmvf2
papergraph.abstract: Solid electrolytes are critical to enabling safe and high-energy-density batteries, yet their practical deployment is impeded by poor electrochemical stability, inadequate interfacial contact, and challenging manufacturing processes. Here, we introduce a novel "solid-in-solid" electrolyte architecture comprising a porous Li zeolite electrolyte (LiX) infiltrated with a melt-processable plastic crystal electrolyte (PCE). This LiX–PCE electrolyte achieves an ionic conductivity of 0.55 mS/cm at 20 °C, alongside improved electrochemical stability over pure PCE. Solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance reveals three Li⁺ transport pathways: through LiX, through PCE, and via ion exchange at phase boundaries. Leveraging the melt-processability of the PCE, we proposed a roll-to-roll-compatible melt infiltration strategy for scalable solid-state battery (SSB) fabrication with the LiX–PCE electrolyte. The SSBs demonstrate excellent rate performance (over 12C), 93% capacity retention after 200 cycles, and 4.5V compatibility. This work elucidates critical design principles for high-performance solid-state electrolytes and presents a viable path toward practical fast-charging, high-power SSBs.
strengths: excellent rate performance (over 12C)
93% capacity retention after 200 cycles
4.5V compatibility
roll-to-roll-compatible melt infiltration strategy
improvements: improved electrochemical stability over pure PCE
papergraph.contribution: novel "solid-in-solid" electrolyte architecture (LiX–PCE) enabling scalable fabrication and high-performance operation in high-voltage solid-state lithium metal batteries
papergraph.limitation: Leveraging the melt-processability of the PCE, we proposed a roll-to-roll-compatible melt infiltration strategy for scalable solid-state battery (SSB) fabrication with the LiX–PCE electrolyte.

papergraph.title: Enabling stability at a high working voltage of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 sodium-ion battery cathode via utilization of succinic anhydride as an electrolyte additive
papergraph.citation: Beatriz Mendoza-Sánchez et al. (2024) Enabling stability at a high working voltage of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 sodium-ion battery cathode via utilization of succinic anhydride as an electrolyte additive doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-41hkj
papergraph.abstract: The practical implementation of P2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 as sodium-ion battery cathode has been hampered by problems of self-discharge and poor cycling stability caused by electrolyte oxidation. Here, we developed an electrolyte formulation that makes use of succinic anhydride as a key additive to alleviate these drawbacks. The composition of the electrolyte was optimized and the so-optimized electrolyte enables a safe performance of the cathode in a 2-4.2 V vs. Na+/Na electrochemical window. Electrochemical studies including electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and microscopy studies showed that succinic anhydride favours the formation of a cathode electrolyte interface that protects the cathode against electrolyte oxidation. As compared to the electrodes tested in the succinic anhydride-free electrolyte, self-discharge processes were substantially reduced and cycling stability (up to 100 cycles, 2-fold improvement) and Coulombic efficiency were improved. More remarkable was the improvement on power density performance. The cathode tested in the optimized electrolyte performed up to the high current density of 5.78C delivering a high power of 1.88 kW kg−1, whereas the cathode tested in the SA-free electrolyte faded readily at 1.77C. Equally, the cathode showed a much more stable average voltage of 3.17-2.89 V vs. Na+/Na, over such a wider range of operating current rates. The cathode delivered a maximum energy density of 377 Wh kg−1. Importantly, SA also aided in preventing corrosion of aluminum current collectors in the electrochemical window of interest.
strengths: reduced self-discharge
2-fold improvement in cycling stability
improved Coulombic efficiency
enhanced power density (1.88 kW kg−1 at 5.78C)
stabilized average voltage over wide current range
prevention of aluminum current collector corrosion
improvements: electrolyte formulation with succinic anhydride additive
optimized electrolyte composition for 2–4.2 V vs. Na+/Na window

papergraph.contribution: Succinic anhydride enables stable high-voltage operation (2–4.2 V vs. Na+/Na) of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 by forming a protective cathode electrolyte interface, reducing self-discharge, improving cycling stability (2×), Coulombic efficiency, power density (1.88 kW kg−1), energy density (377 Wh kg−1), and preventing Al corrosion.

papergraph.title: Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery
papergraph.citation: Ritesh Kumar et al. (2025) Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-7nnbl
papergraph.abstract: Electrolyte development for next-generation batteries has largely relied on knowledge-driven trial-and-error. A significant reason for this approach is due to the vast, high-dimensional molecular design space for solvents and molecular mixtures. Popular dimensionality reduction techniques such as principal component analysis (PCA), t-distributed stochastic neighbor embeddings (t-SNE), and uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP) can facilitate chemical exploration by projecting this space into low dimensions, yet their applicability in electrolyte discovery remains underexplored. Here, we present a framework to assess the faithfulness of various dimensionality reduction methods in preserving chemically meaningful relationships. Then, we apply this framework to guide electrolyte solvent discovery by searching for electrolyte solvents with high cosine similarity to state-of-the-art systems, followed by formulation refinement using domain knowledge. We then performed experimental validation which revealed different failure modes for different electrolyte selections. We specifically focus on next generation lithium metal batteries (LMBs), and identified a non-fluorinated ether electrolyte solvent that exhibited high Coulombic efficiency (CE). Our findings demonstrate that rational navigation of the electrolyte chemical space, enabled by dimensionality reduction and guided by data, can complement traditional approaches and accelerate the discovery of advanced battery materials.

papergraph.limitation: Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections.

papergraph.title: A new perspective on the development of stable, high-power-density 5 V-class all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries
papergraph.citation: Zhili Liang et al. (2026) A new perspective on the development of stable, high-power-density 5 V-class all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv.10001819/v2
papergraph.abstract: The correlation between the electrochemical performance and the electronic structure and chemical composition of the cathode/electrolyte-and anode/electrolyte-interfaces in all-solid-state batteries (ASSB) is systematically studied. ASSBs are composed of LiCoPO4 (LCP) or LiCoO2 (redox potential ~4.8 V and ~3.8 V vs Li+/Li, respectively) thin-film cathode materials, Li1+xAlxTi2-x(PO4)3 (LATP) as the solid-state electrolyte and lithium metal as the anode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) interface experiments on LCP thin-film deposition on the LATP surface reveal a partial reduction of Ti4+ ions attributed to electronic charge transfer from LCP to LATP without the involvement of the PO4 polyanion in the process. Electrochemical activity of the ASSBs is primarily limited by the anode/electrolyte interface rather than the cathode/electrolyte interface. Post-cycling XPS analysis of the Li∣LATP interface indicates lateral and in-depth chemical inhomogeneity with a strong change in PO4 polyanionic chemical environment and Li+ accumulation at the areas with a better electrolyte/anode contact. Engineering the Li∣LATP interface via coating of the LATP surface with lithium oxynitride (LiPON) or a LiTFSI-PEO polymer electrolyte, or by wetting the LATP with the liquid electrolytes, significantly improves battery cycling stability even without artificial interface modification at the cathode side. The optimized Li∣LiTFSI-PEO∣LATP∣LCP cells showed excellent cycling performance between 3.0 V and 5.0 V at a charging time of 12 min (≈ 5C rate). Stable cycling was sustained over 10 cycles, followed by 30 and 50 additional cycles at reduced charging rates (≈ 4C and 1C, respectively). Good electrochemical cyclability was further demonstrated with an upper cut-off voltage of 5.3 V vs. Li+/Li.
strengths: accumulation at the areas with a better electrolyte/anode contact. Engineering the Li∣LATP interface via coating of the LATP surface with lithium oxynitride (LiPON) or a LiTFSI-PEO polymer electrolyte, or by wetting the LATP with the liquid electrolytes, significantly improves battery cycling stabil...[truncated]
papergraph.contribution: Electrochemical activity of ASSBs is primarily limited by the anode/electrolyte interface rather than the cathode/electrolyte interface; interface engineering of Li∣LATP (e.g., via LiPON or LiTFSI-PEO coating) significantly improves cycling stability
papergraph.limitation: Electrochemical activity of the ASSBs is primarily limited by the anode/electrolyte interface

evidence.contribution: FSSE effectively prevents release of crystal water from PB cathode while enabling superior cyclability in K||PB cells
evidence.limitation: Abstract: Prussian blue (PB) and its analogues are emerging cathode materials for potassium batteries due to their 3D open framework allowing facile ion diffusion. However, the crystal water, an inherent component of PB structure, can be released into the electrolytes especially during high voltage...[truncated]

evidence.title: Highly Antioxidative Lithium Salt Enables High-Voltage Ether Electrolyte for Lithium Metal Battery
evidence.citation: Jian He et al. (2024) Highly Antioxidative Lithium Salt Enables High-Voltage Ether Electrolyte for Lithium Metal Battery arXiv:2409.18344v1
evidence.contribution: LiPFBS enables stable high-voltage operation of ether-based electrolyte via formation of robust, inorganic-dominant CEI layer
evidence.limitation: A PFBS$^{-}$-derived CEI significantly enhances the overall performance of the full cell under high voltage conditions.

evidence.title: Deciphering Interphase Instability of Lithium Metal Batteries with Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes at Elevated Temperatures
evidence.citation: Tao Meng et al. (2024) Deciphering Interphase Instability of Lithium Metal Batteries with Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes at Elevated Temperatures arXiv:2401.05671v1
evidence.benchmark: capacity retention | CEI stability | microcracking suppression
evidence.contribution: Identification of fluorine radical–driven solvent decomposition as the root cause of CEI reconstruction and cyclability degradation in LHCE-based LMBs at elevated temperatures; demonstration that DODSi acid scavenger suppresses microcracking and enables 93.0% capacity retention after 100 cycles at 80 °C.
evidence.limitation: Here, we decipher the interphase instability in LHCE-based LMBs with a Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathode at elevated temperatures.

evidence.title: Solid-in-Solid" Electrolyte via Scalable Melting Infiltration Method for High-Voltage Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries
evidence.citation: Tongtai Ji et al. (2025) "Solid-in-Solid" Electrolyte via Scalable Melting Infiltration Method for High-Voltage Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-hmvf2
evidence.contribution: novel "solid-in-solid" electrolyte architecture (LiX–PCE) enabling scalable fabrication and high-performance operation in high-voltage solid-state lithium metal batteries
evidence.limitation: Leveraging the melt-processability of the PCE, we proposed a roll-to-roll-compatible melt infiltration strategy for scalable solid-state battery (SSB) fabrication with the LiX–PCE electrolyte.

evidence.title: Enabling stability at a high working voltage of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 sodium-ion battery cathode via utilization of succinic anhydride as an electrolyte additive
evidence.citation: Beatriz Mendoza-Sánchez et al. (2024) Enabling stability at a high working voltage of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 sodium-ion battery cathode via utilization of succinic anhydride as an electrolyte additive doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-41hkj
evidence.method: electrolyte formulation with succinic anhydride additive
evidence.contribution: Succinic anhydride enables stable high-voltage operation (2–4.2 V vs. Na+/Na) of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 by forming a protective cathode electrolyte interface, reducing self-discharge, improving cycling stability (2×), Coulombic efficiency, power density (1.88 kW kg−1), energy density (377 Wh kg−1), and preventing Al corrosion.
evidence.limitation: As compared to the electrodes tested in the succinic anhydride-free electrolyte, self-discharge processes were substantially reduced and cycling stability (up to 100 cycles, 2-fold improvement) and Coulombic efficiency were improved.

evidence.title: Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery
evidence.citation: Ritesh Kumar et al. (2025) Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-7nnbl
evidence.scenario: battery electrolyte solvent discovery for lithium metal batteries
evidence.contribution: A framework to assess faithfulness of dimensionality reduction methods for preserving chemically meaningful relationships in electrolyte solvent space, enabling data-guided discovery of high-performing non-fluorinated ether electrolytes for lithium metal batteries.
evidence.limitation: Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections.

evidence.title: A new perspective on the development of stable, high-power-density 5 V-class all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries
evidence.citation: Zhili Liang et al. (2026) A new perspective on the development of stable, high-power-density 5 V-class all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv.10001819/v2
evidence.contribution: Electrochemical activity of ASSBs is primarily limited by the anode/electrolyte interface rather than the cathode/electrolyte interface; interface engineering of Li∣LATP (e.g., via LiPON or LiTFSI-PEO coating) significantly improves cycling stability
evidence.limitation: Electrochemical activity of the ASSBs is primarily limited by the anode/electrolyte interface

hypotheses.statement: If the conflicting claims in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries are retested under matched the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims conditions, then Coulombic efficiency (CE) will reveal which mechanism is valid and where the disagreement boundary lies: 100 cycles.
hypotheses.mechanism: This proposal addresses the PaperGraph gap 'Potential conclusion conflict: all-solid-state lithium metal batteries contains opposing claims: Li|Li symmetric cell cycling showed very stable and reversible cycling of Li metal over 100 cycles and smooth, dendrite-free deposition morphology. vs Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections..'. It hypothesizes a testable mechanism: Concrete mechanism chain: (1) method capability: X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) with in vacuo scratching contributes through turning a latent physical, chemical, or biological state into a calibrated observable signal; (2) scenario target: in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries, the affected process is a measurable material, molecular, or reaction mechanism under controlled conditions; (3) measurable consequence: the bridge must produce a preregistered change in Coulombic efficiency (CE). The method's input requirements are broad or not clearly specified; the experiment must make them explicit. The required bridge is benchmark dataset, simulation. The decisive prediction is that this concrete bridge, not a generic representation change, will alter Coulombic efficiency (CE); if the bridge data or causal link is absent, the hypothesis should fail rather than be reinterpreted post hoc. MingLi treats this as a structural mutation rather than a surface rephrasing. The study is designed to be falsifiable through Build a minimal benchmark for all-solid-state lithium metal batteries; compare X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) with in vacuo scratching against canonical baselines; measure Coulombic efficiency (CE); include negative controls, ablations, and failure-mode analysis.

keynotes.title: A review and outlook on anionic and cationic redox in Ni-, Li- and Mn-rich layered oxides LiMeO2 (Me = Li, Ni, Co, Mn)

keynote.title: A review and outlook on anionic and cationic redox in Ni-, Li- and Mn-rich layered oxides LiMeO2 (Me = Li, Ni, Co, Mn)
keynote.core_problem: Understanding the dual redox mechanisms (anionic vs. cationic) in Ni-based layered oxide cathodes and their impact on electrochemical performance and structural stability.
contributions: Systematic review linking charge compensation mechanisms to electronic and crystallographic structural changes in LiNi1-xMexO2 (x <= 0.2, Me = Co, Mn)

Differentiation of two redox regimes: oxygen-centered redox at low–medium SOC (O 2p → Ni eg sigma bonding), and transition metal → oxygen electron transfer at high SOC (enabling pi-bonding but triggering oxygen release)
Correlation of Ni:Co:Mn composition with voltage profile features and performance metrics (capacity, cycling stability, open-circuit voltage)
methods: Literature review and mechanistic synthesis
Analysis of electronic structure changes (sigma vs. pi bonding formation)
Correlation of compositional variation with electrochemical behavior
assumptions: Redox activity can be partitioned into distinct SOC-dependent regimes
Oxygen redox is reversible only up to medium SOC; irreversible at high SOC due to O2 release
Ni:Co:Mn ratio dominantly governs observed voltage profiles and degradation trends
limitations: No quantitative modeling or first-principles calculations presented
Lack of direct experimental evidence (e.g., in situ XAS, RIXS, or neutron PDF) to unambiguously assign bonding changes
Scope limited to R-3m structured LiNi1-xMexO2 with x <= 0.2; excludes Li-rich layered oxides with monoclinic C2/m symmetry where anionic redox is more prominent

gap_signals.text: Lack of direct experimental evidence (e.g., in situ XAS, RIXS, or neutron PDF) to unambiguously assign bonding changes
No quantitative modeling or first-principles calculations presented
Scope limited to R-3m structured LiNi1-xMexO2 with x <= 0.2; excludes Li-rich layered oxides with monoclinic C2/m symmetry where anionic redox is more prominent
Relies on qualitative electronic structure interpretation without spectroscopic or computational validation.
Excludes Li-rich layered oxides (e.g., xLi2MnO3·(1−x)LiMO2) with monoclinic C2/m symmetry, where anionic redox is more dominant and structurally distinct.

datasets_or_materials: LiNi1-xMexO2 (x <= 0.2, Me = Co, Mn), R-3m layered oxides

important_claims.claim: At low–medium states of charge, charge compensation occurs primarily at oxygen sites via electron density shift from oxygen lattice to nickel, forming sigma bonds.
important_claims.evidence: Described as observed mechanism in Ni-based layered oxides; tied to voltage profile features and performance parameters.

important_claims.claim: At high states of charge, electron density shifts from transition metals to oxygen, enabling pi-bond formation but also causing oxygen release and detrimental side reactions.
important_claims.evidence: Linked to capacity loss and cycling instability; described as fundamental cause of degradation in high-SOC operation.

important_claims.claim: Capacity, cycling stability, and open-circuit voltage are functions of Ni:Co:Mn composition.
important_claims.evidence: Stated explicitly in full-text excerpt: 'performance parameters like the capacity, the cycling stability and the open cell voltage become a function of the composition.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a conceptual framework for mapping redox activity to SOC windows and compositional design rules in conventional Ni-rich cathodes; useful for guiding targeted experiments (e.g., operando spectroscopy) or DFT studies on sigma/pi bonding evolution.

keynotes.title: Cathode Materials for Lithium Ion Batteries (LIBs): A Review on Materials related aspects towards High Energy Density LIBs

keynote.title: Cathode Materials for Lithium Ion Batteries (LIBs): A Review on Materials related aspects towards High Energy Density LIBs
keynote.core_problem: Achieving high energy density in lithium-ion batteries while maintaining cyclic, structural, and thermal stability, and ensuring high ionic/electronic conductivity across cathode materials.
contributions: Chronological classification of cathode materials into three generations
Systematic review of material evolution from LiCoO₂ to complex high-lithium systems (e.g., Li₂TMSiO₄)
Identification of key challenges: cyclic stability, structural/thermal stability, high operating voltage, and charge transport efficiency

gap_signals.text: Numerous challenges such as the cyclic stability of cathode materials, their structural and thermal stability, higher operating voltage together with high ionic and electronic conductivity for efficient ion and charge transport during charging and discharging.

important_claims.claim: Ternary oxides such as LiTMOₓ are first-generation cathode materials.
important_claims.evidence: Stated in abstract: 'The ternary oxides such as LiTMOx (TM=Transition Metal) are considered as the first generation materials'

important_claims.claim: Third-generation cathode materials include complex oxide systems with higher lithium content (e.g., Li₂TMSiO₄) aiming for higher energy density.
important_claims.evidence: Stated in abstract: 'The current i.e. third generation includes complex oxide systems with higher lithium content such as Li₂TMSiO₄ aiming for higher energy density.'

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generational taxonomy and historical context for cathode material design; useful for identifying evolutionary trends, benchmarking new materials against generational milestones, and framing research questions around stability–performance trade-offs.

keynotes.title: Cathode Materials for Lithium Ion Batteries (LIBs): A Review on Materials related aspects towards High Energy Density LIBs

keynote.title: Cathode Materials for Lithium Ion Batteries (LIBs): A Review on Materials related aspects towards High Energy Density LIBs
keynote.core_problem: Achieving high energy density in lithium-ion batteries while maintaining cyclic, structural, and thermal stability, high operating voltage, and high ionic/electronic conductivity.
contributions: Chronological classification of cathode materials into three generations

keynotes.title: Mechanistic Study of Functional-Solvent-Based Electrolytes for High Voltage Lithium Batteries

keynote.core_problem: Rapid capacity decay of Ni-rich cathode (e.g., NMC811) based Li-ion batteries at high cutoff voltages (>4.5 V vs Li/Li+) due to conventional carbonate electrolyte decomposition, parasitic oxidation at cathode/electrolyte interface, irreversible cathode phase changes, and transition metal dissolution.

gap_signals.text: Fundamental mechanisms underlying functional solvent-induced interfacial stability remain unclear.

important_claims.claim: Some unique combinations of functional solvents lead to exceptionally stable cycle performance in Ni-rich cathode-based LIBs.
important_claims.evidence: Reported observation in the study; supported by electrochemical cycling data and interfacial characterization.

important_claims.claim: Functional-solvent-based electrolytes suppress electrolyte decomposition and parasitic oxidation at high voltage.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides reusable solvent design principles and characterization protocol (XPS + DFT) for high-voltage electrolyte development; enables systematic screening of functional solvent families beyond fluorinated carbonates and organosilicons.

keynotes.title: Electrolyte stability and instability in lithium-ion batteries

keynote.title: Electrolyte stability and instability in lithium-ion batteries
keynote.core_problem: Electrolyte instability—particularly ethylene carbonate (EC) decomposition—at electrode-electrolyte interfaces in Ni-rich NMC/graphite lithium-ion batteries, leading to poor capacity retention.
contributions: Identifies EC as a key contributor to cathode-side degradation in Ni-rich NMC cells
Demonstrates that EC-free electrolytes improve performance via enhanced interfacial stability at both cathode and anode
Links suppressed solvent oxidation at the cathode to reduced generation and crossover of acidic species
Shows graphite solid-electrolyte interphase (SEI) in EC-free systems is more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition
methods: Electrochemical methods
Operando pressure measurements
Post-mortem X-ray analysis
Post-mortem solution NMR studies
experiments_or_evidence: Comparison of Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing vs. EC-free electrolytes
Capacity retention metrics showing improved performance with EC-free electrolytes
Evidence of suppressed solvent oxidation at cathode interface (via electrochemical & operando pressure data)
Evidence of reduced acidic species formation and crossover (via NMR and interfacial analysis)
Evidence of enhanced graphite SEI resilience against acid attack (via post-mortem X-ray and NMR)

gap_signals.text: Prior studies lacked combined operando pressure + post-mortem NMR/X-ray to resolve coupled cathode/anode degradation pathways.
EC’s role in Ni-rich cathode degradation was previously under-characterized at the mechanistic level across both electrodes.

important_claims.claim: Improved performance in EC-free electrolytes arises from improved stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces.
important_claims.evidence: Combined electrochemical, operando pressure, and post-mortem X-ray/NMR data across full cells.

important_claims.claim: Suppressed solvent oxidation at the cathode reduces formation and crossover of acidic species.
important_claims.evidence: Operando pressure trends and solution NMR detection of acidic degradation products.

important_claims.claim: Graphite SEI in EC-free systems is more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition.
important_claims.evidence: Post-mortem X-ray and NMR analyses of anode interfaces after cycling.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a multi-modal experimental framework (electrochemistry + operando pressure + multimodal post-mortem analysis) for deconvolving coupled interfacial degradation in advanced Li-ion chemistries; identifies EC-free formulation design principles transferable to other Ni-rich or high-voltage cathode systems.

keynotes.title: A free-standing sulfone-based solid-state electrolyte mitigating the release of crystal water from the Prussian blue cathode for high voltage potassium batteries

keynote.title: A free-standing sulfone-based solid-state electrolyte mitigating the release of crystal water from the Prussian blue cathode for high voltage potassium batteries
keynote.core_problem: Crystal water release from Prussian blue (PB) cathodes during high-voltage operation causes side reactions and rapid degradation in potassium batteries.
contributions: Design and demonstration of a free-standing sulfone-based solid-state electrolyte (FSSE) that blocks crystal water release from PB cathodes
Achievement of exceptional oxidative stability (~4.8 V vs. K+/K) and decent ionic conductivity (2.2 × 10⁻⁴ S cm⁻¹ at 25 °C)
Demonstration of improved cyclability in K||PB cells (74.6% capacity retention over 200 cycles) versus liquid carbonate electrolyte (53.3%)
methods: Synthesis of free-standing sulfone-based solid-state electrolyte (FSSE) using potassium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide, dimethyl sulfone, and UV-cured trimethylolpropane ethoxylate triacrylate network
Electrochemical characterization including ionic conductivity measurement, oxidative stability assessment, and galvanostatic cycling
experiments_or_evidence: Comparative cycling performance of K||PB cells with FSSE vs. liquid carbonate electrolyte (74.6% vs. 53.3% capacity retention after 200 cycles)
Measurement of ionic conductivity (2.2 × 10⁻⁴ S cm⁻¹ at 25 °C)
Oxidative stability evaluation (~4.8 V vs. K+/K)

gap_signals.text: Crystal water release from PB cathodes during high-voltage operation remains unmitigated in conventional electrolytes.

important_claims.claim: FSSE effectively prevents the release of crystal water from PB cathode.
important_claims.evidence: Superior cyclability and stability of K||PB cells with FSSE compared to liquid carbonate electrolyte; implied functional validation via suppressed degradation.

important_claims.claim: FSSE exhibits suitable electrolyte properties: decent ionic conductivity, exceptional oxidative stability, and intimate contact with porous PB electrodes.
important_claims.evidence: Reported ionic conductivity of 2.2 × 10⁻⁴ S cm⁻¹ at 25 °C, oxidative stability ~4.8 V vs. K+/K, and successful integration with porous composite PB electrodes.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a design principle—water-blocking solid-state electrolytes—for stabilizing hydrated cathode materials (e.g., PB analogues) in high-voltage alkali-metal batteries; enables exploration of otherwise unstable hydrated frameworks.

methods: Electrolyte formulation: LiPFBS in DME vs. benchmark LiTFSI/DME
Interphase characterization (SEI/CEI): likely XPS, TEM, or FTIR (inferred from claims but not specified in provided text)
Electrochemical evaluation in Li-LiCoO2 full cells at 4.6 V
experiments_or_evidence: Stable cycling of Li-LiCoO2 full cell at 4.6 V using LiPFBS/DME
Comparative analysis of SEI and CEI formation versus LiTFSI/DME
Evidence of inorganic-dominant CEI derived from PFBS⁻
assumptions: DME’s Li metal compatibility is preserved under high-voltage operation when paired with LiPFBS
PFBS⁻ anion oxidation products selectively form protective CEI without compromising SEI integrity

gap_signals.text: Existing LiTFSI/DME systems fail to stabilize DME above ~4.3 V, necessitating new salt design.
Ether electrolytes are incompatible with high-voltage cathodes due to oxidative decomposition.

important_claims.claim: A PFBS⁻-derived CEI significantly enhances the overall performance of the full cell under high voltage conditions.
important_claims.evidence: Full-cell operation at 4.6 V with improved stability compared to LiTFSI/DME baseline.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a design principle—using antioxidative anions to engineer cathode interphases—for extending ether electrolytes to high-voltage applications; enables reuse of DME-based formulations in next-generation Li metal batteries.

keynotes.title: Deciphering Interphase Instability of Lithium Metal Batteries with Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes at Elevated Temperatures

keynote.title: Deciphering Interphase Instability of Lithium Metal Batteries with Localized High-Concentration Electrolytes at Elevated Temperatures
keynote.core_problem: Interphase instability in localized high-concentration electrolyte (LHCE)-based lithium metal batteries (LMBs) with Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 cathodes at elevated temperatures, leading to poor cyclability and safety risks.
contributions: Identification of fluorine radicals as key drivers of solvent decomposition and CEI reconstruction in LHCE at high temperature
Demonstration that dimethoxydimethylsilane (DODSi) acts as an acid scavenger to suppress microcracking and stabilize the cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI)
Achievement of 93.0% capacity retention after 100 cycles at 80 °C in Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2||Li cells using DODSi-functionalized LHCE
methods: Electrochemical cycling of Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2||Li full cells at 80 °C
Analysis of interphase chemistry (implied via post-mortem characterization, though specific techniques not named in excerpt)
Introduction and evaluation of dimethoxydimethylsilane (DODSi) as an acid scavenger additive
experiments_or_evidence: Capacity retention of 93.0% after 100 cycles at 80 °C for Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2||Li cells with DODSi-functionalized LHCE
Evidence of suppressed CEI microcracking with DODSi addition
Link established between fluorine radical generation, solvent decomposition, and CEI reconstruction

gap_signals.text: No existing strategy to mitigate fluorine-radical-induced solvent decomposition and CEI degradation in LHCE at high temperature
Lack of stable interphases in LHCE-based LMBs under elevated temperature and high-voltage conditions

important_claims.claim: Fluorine radicals generated in the LHCE induce solvent decomposition and reconstruct the CEI, degrading cyclability.
important_claims.evidence: Findings reveal fluorine radical generation leads to solvent decomposition and consequent chain reactions that reconstruct the CEI and degrade battery cyclability.

important_claims.claim: DODSi as an acid scavenger significantly boosts CEI stability and suppresses microcracking.
important_claims.evidence: As further evidenced, introducing DODSi significantly boosts CEI stability with suppressed microcracking.

important_claims.claim: DODSi-functionalized LHCE enables unprecedented high-temperature cycling stability.
important_claims.evidence: A Ni0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2||Li cell with this DODSi-functionalized LHCE achieves 93.0% capacity retention after 100 cycles at 80 °C.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a mechanistic insight (fluorine radical pathway) and a reusable electrolyte design principle (acid scavenging) for stabilizing CEI in high-voltage, high-temperature LMBs; DODSi or analogous scavengers may be transferable to other LHCE or fluoride-rich electrolyte systems.

keynotes.title: Solid-in-Solid" Electrolyte via Scalable Melting Infiltration Method for High-Voltage Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries

keynote.title: Solid-in-Solid" Electrolyte via Scalable Melting Infiltration Method for High-Voltage Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries
keynote.core_problem: Poor electrochemical stability, inadequate interfacial contact, and challenging manufacturing processes impede practical deployment of solid electrolytes in high-voltage solid-state lithium metal batteries.
contributions: Novel "solid-in-solid" electrolyte architecture combining porous Li zeolite (LiX) and melt-processable plastic crystal electrolyte (PCE)
Roll-to-roll-compatible melt infiltration fabrication strategy
Elucidation of three Li⁺ transport pathways via solid-state NMR
methods: Melt infiltration of PCE into porous LiX scaffold
Solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (ssNMR) for Li⁺ transport pathway analysis
experiments_or_evidence: Ionic conductivity of 0.55 mS/cm at 20 °C
Improved electrochemical stability over pure PCE
SSB rate performance >12C
93% capacity retention after 200 cycles
4.5V compatibility

gap_signals.text: Challenging manufacturing processes.
Need for high-voltage (4.5V), fast-charging (>12C), and long-cycle-life (93% retention after 200 cycles) solid-state batteries.
Practical deployment impeded by poor electrochemical stability, inadequate interfacial contact, and challenging manufacturing processes.

important_claims.claim: LiX–PCE electrolyte achieves ionic conductivity of 0.55 mS/cm at 20 °C.
important_claims.evidence: Directly reported measurement.

important_claims.claim: Three Li⁺ transport pathways exist: through LiX, through PCE, and via ion exchange at phase boundaries.
important_claims.evidence: Revealed by solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance.

important_claims.claim: SSBs with LiX–PCE electrolyte show 4.5V compatibility, >12C rate capability, and 93% capacity retention after 200 cycles.
important_claims.evidence: Reported experimental battery performance metrics.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a scalable fabrication method (melt infiltration) and mechanistic insight (multi-pathway Li⁺ transport) applicable to design and processing of composite solid electrolytes; architecture and processing strategy are transferable to other zeolite/plastic crystal or analogous solid-in-solid systems.

keynotes.title: Enabling stability at a high working voltage of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 sodium-ion battery cathode via utilization of succinic anhydride as an electrolyte additive

keynote.title: Enabling stability at a high working voltage of aP2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 sodium-ion battery cathode via utilization of succinic anhydride as an electrolyte additive
keynote.core_problem: Self-discharge and poor cycling stability of P2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 cathode due to electrolyte oxidation at high voltage (up to 4.2 V vs. Na+/Na), compounded by aluminum current collector corrosion.
contributions: Introduction and optimization of succinic anhydride (SA) as an electrolyte additive for P2-type sodium-ion cathodes
Demonstration of improved cycling stability (2-fold increase over 100 cycles), reduced self-discharge, and enhanced Coulombic efficiency
Achievement of high-power operation (5.78C, 1.88 kW kg−1) and stable average voltage (3.17–2.89 V) across current rates
Evidence that SA promotes formation of a protective cathode electrolyte interphase (CEI) inhibiting electrolyte oxidation
Mitigation of aluminum current collector corrosion in the 2–4.2 V vs. Na+/Na window
methods: Electrolyte formulation optimization with succinic anhydride (SA)
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)
Microscopy studies (method unspecified, e.g., SEM/TEM/XPS implied but not named)
Galvanostatic cycling at varied current densities (including up to 5.78C)
Self-discharge and Coulombic efficiency evaluation
experiments_or_evidence: 2-fold improvement in cycling stability over 100 cycles with SA vs. SA-free electrolyte
Suppressed self-discharge in SA-containing electrolyte
Sustained performance at 5.78C (1.88 kW kg−1 power density) vs. rapid fade at 1.77C without SA
Stable average voltage range of 3.17–2.89 V vs. Na+/Na across current rates with SA
Maximum energy density of 377 Wh kg−1 achieved with SA
EIS and microscopy evidence of CEI formation enabling protection against electrolyte oxidation
Observed prevention of aluminum current collector corrosion in 2–4.2 V window

gap_signals.text: Lack of understanding of CEI formation mechanism on P2 cathodes under high-voltage operation
P2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 cathode practical implementation hampered by self-discharge and poor cycling stability
No prior use or evaluation of succinic anhydride for stabilizing P2-type Na-ion cathodes at high voltage

datasets_or_materials: Succinic anhydride (SA)-containing and SA-free carbonate-based electrolytes (exact base composition unspecified)

important_claims.claim: Succinic anhydride enables safe operation of P2-Na0.85Li0.17Ni0.21Mn0.64O2 in 2–4.2 V vs. Na+/Na window.
important_claims.evidence: Cycling stability up to 100 cycles, stable voltage profile, and absence of Al corrosion in that window with SA.

important_claims.claim: SA promotes formation of a protective cathode electrolyte interface (CEI).
important_claims.evidence: Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and microscopy studies showed SA favors CEI formation that protects against electrolyte oxidation.

important_claims.claim: SA improves power capability dramatically.
important_claims.evidence: Cathode delivers 5.78C (1.88 kW kg−1) with SA, while fading readily at 1.77C without SA.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: High: Provides a validated, low-cost electrolyte additive strategy for stabilizing high-voltage P2-type layered oxide cathodes; mechanistic CEI insights transferable to other Na-ion systems; methodology applicable to screening of anhydride-based additives.

keynotes.title: Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery

keynote.title: Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery
keynote.core_problem: Electrolyte development for next-generation batteries relies on trial-and-error due to the vast, high-dimensional molecular design space for solvents and mixtures.
contributions: A framework to assess the faithfulness of dimensionality reduction methods in preserving chemically meaningful relationships
Application of the framework to guide electrolyte solvent discovery via cosine similarity search against state-of-the-art systems
Formulation refinement using domain knowledge
Experimental validation identifying distinct failure modes across electrolyte selections
Identification of a non-fluorinated ether electrolyte solvent with high Coulombic efficiency for lithium metal batteries
methods: Principal component analysis (PCA)
t-distributed stochastic neighbor embedding (t-SNE)
Uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP)
Cosine similarity search in reduced solvent embedding space
Domain-knowledge-guided formulation refinement
experiments_or_evidence: Experimental validation revealing different failure modes for different electrolyte selections
Measurement of Coulombic efficiency (CE) for identified non-fluorinated ether solvent in lithium metal batteries

gap_signals.text: Lack of frameworks to assess faithfulness of dimensionality reduction methods in preserving chemically meaningful relationships.
Dimensionality reduction applicability in electrolyte discovery remains underexplored.

important_claims.claim: Rational navigation of the electrolyte chemical space, enabled by dimensionality reduction and guided by data, can complement traditional approaches and accelerate discovery.
important_claims.evidence: Experimental validation including identification of a non-fluorinated ether solvent with high Coulombic efficiency and observation of distinct failure modes across selections.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a generalizable framework for evaluating and applying chemical embeddings in materials discovery—particularly where domain-specific relational fidelity (e.g., electrochemical compatibility, stability) is critical—beyond battery electrolytes.

keynotes.title: A new perspective on the development of stable, high-power-density 5 V-class all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries

keynote.title: A new perspective on the development of stable, high-power-density 5 V-class all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries
keynote.core_problem: Electrochemical instability and limited cycling performance in 5 V-class all-solid-state lithium-ion batteries, primarily driven by interfacial degradation at the anode/electrolyte interface.
contributions: Identifies the anode/electrolyte (Li∣LATP) interface—not the cathode/electrolyte interface—as the primary bottleneck for electrochemical activity in 5 V-class ASSBs.
Reveals Ti4+ partial reduction at the LCP∣LATP interface via XPS, indicating electronic charge transfer independent of PO4 polyanion involvement.
Demonstrates that interfacial chemical inhomogeneity (especially in PO4 environment and Li+ accumulation) at the Li∣LATP interface correlates with poor cycling stability.

Shows that engineering the Li∣LATP interface—via LiPON coating, LiTFSI-PEO polymer layer, or liquid electrolyte wetting—enables stable high-voltage (up to 5.3 V) and high-rate (≈5C) cycling without cathode-side interface modification.
methods: Thin-film fabrication of LiCoPO4 (LCP) and LiCoO2 cathodes
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) for interfacial chemical analysis (pre- and post-cycling)
Electrochemical cycling tests across multiple rates (≈5C, ≈4C, 1C) and voltage windows (3.0–5.0 V, up to 5.3 V)
experiments_or_evidence: XPS interface experiments showing partial reduction of Ti4+ at LCP∣LATP interface
Post-cycling XPS revealing lateral/in-depth chemical inhomogeneity and altered PO4 environment at Li∣LATP interface
Cycling stability improvement using LiPON, LiTFSI-PEO, or liquid electrolyte at Li∣LATP interface
10-cycle stable cycling at ≈5C (12 min charge), followed by 30 cycles at ≈4C and 50 cycles at 1C in optimized Li∣LiTFSI-PEO∣LATP∣LCP cells
Demonstration of cycling up to 5.3 V vs. Li+/Li
assumptions: Electronic charge transfer at LCP∣LATP interface occurs without PO4 participation
Chemical inhomogeneity observed at Li∣LATP interface is causally linked to electrochemical degradation
Interface engineering strategies (coating, polymer layer, wetting) mitigate interfacial reactivity without altering bulk electrolyte properties
limitations: Limited cycle count reported (total ≤90 cycles); long-term (>100 cycles) stability not demonstrated
Thin-film geometry used—may not generalize to practical composite electrodes or scalable manufacturing
No quantification of interfacial resistance evolution (e.g., EIS) across cycling
No direct measurement of Li metal morphology or dendrite formation

gap_signals.text: Limited cycle count reported (total ≤90 cycles); long-term (>100 cycles) stability not demonstrated
No direct measurement of Li metal morphology or dendrite formation
No quantification of interfacial resistance evolution (e.g., EIS) across cycling
Thin-film geometry used—may not generalize to practical composite electrodes or scalable manufacturing
Results based exclusively on thin-film model systems; applicability to thick, composite cathodes or commercial-grade LATP pellets remains unverified.
No direct evidence linking PO4 chemical environment changes to specific degradation products (e.g., Li3PO4, reduced phosphates) or interfacial conductivity loss.
Absence of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) or operando characterization to track interfacial resistance or phase evolution during cycling.

important_claims.claim: Electrochemical activity of the ASSBs is primarily limited by the anode/electrolyte interface rather than the cathode/electrolyte interface.
important_claims.evidence: Post-cycling XPS analysis and comparative interface engineering outcomes show degradation signatures and performance gains localized at Li∣LATP, not LCP∣LATP.

important_claims.claim: Engineering the Li∣LATP interface significantly improves battery cycling stability even without artificial interface modification at the cathode side.
important_claims.evidence: Optimized Li∣LiTFSI-PEO∣LATP∣LCP cells achieve stable cycling up to 5.3 V and ≈5C rate solely through anode-side interface modification.

keynote.reuse_value_for_research: Provides a validated thin-film ASSB platform and interfacial diagnostic protocol (XPS + voltage/rate-resolved cycling) for probing anode/electrolyte interface stability in high-voltage solid-state systems; highlights LATP–Li metal incompatibility as a key design constraint.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: review of charge compensation mechanisms
Systematic review linking anionic and cationic redox mechanisms to structural and electronic changes in layered oxide cathodes, and their impact on electrochemical performance.

Depending on the Ni:Co:Mn content, both processes lead to characteristic features in the voltage profile of the cathode materials and performance parameters like the capacity, the cycling stability and the open cell voltage become a function of the composition.
Full text excerpt:

Upon charge and discharge two fundamentally different redox mechanisms are observed: At low and medium states of charge (SOCs) charge compensation takes mainly place at oxygen sites while electron density is shifted from the oxygen lattice to nickel (formation of sigma bonds).

Identification of Ni2+/3+ and Ni3+/4+ redox couples via Ni 2p spectra as a function of voltage; observation of Fermi level shift via Co 2p and O 1s spectra; evidence of non-cationic redox at high voltage (4.8 V) via absence of Ni 2p spectral change despite full Li deintercalation; voltage-dependent...[truncated]
XPS is inherently limited to a low information depth, especially problematic due to CEI layer formation on cathodes
Full text excerpt:
X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) is a promising method to gain information on the electronic structure while inherently limited to a low information depth.

In our previous studies, we developed an in vacuo scratching method, which allowed the removal of a large fraction of the cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) and applied the method to the cathode materials LiCoO2 (LCO) and LiNi0.3Mn0.3Co0.3O2 (NMC333) to investigate the charge state dependent transi...[truncated]

In this study, the in vacuo scratching method was applied to the Ni-rich layered oxide compounds, including LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2 (NMC622), LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811) and 2 LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA).
Based on the Co 2p and O 1s photoelectron spectra, a shift to lower binding energies was observed due to the downward shift of the Fermi level.

Li+/Li resulted in fully deintercalated compounds, as shown by the Li 1s photoelectron spectra, while no further change in the Ni 2p photoelectron spectra suggests deviations from the cationic redox process.

Abstract: The pervasive use of Ni-rich cathode active materials, e.g., LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811), for high energy density Li-ion batteries (LIBs) has been hindered by the rapid capacity decay when cycled at high cutoff voltages, due to electrolyte decomposition of the conventional carbonate solve...[truncated]

These HVE-based batteries demonstrated a significant improvement in cycle stability when operated at a charge cutoff voltage of 4.5 V (vs Li/Li+).
We will show that some unique combinations of the functional solvents could lead to exceptionally stable cycle performance in the Ni-rich cathode-based LIBs.

We will discuss possible fundamental mechanisms responsible for the observed performance stability of the high-voltage LIBs using ex-situ XPS, density functional theory calculations, and other characterization techniques.

evidence_type_annotations.claim: electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, post-mortem X-ray, solution NMR

EC-free electrolytes improve capacity retention via enhanced stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces—suppressing solvent oxidation at the cathode and yielding a graphite SEI more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition.

In this letter, electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, and post-mortem X-ray and solution NMR studies were combined to investigate electrolyte degradation phenomena in Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes.
Full text excerpt:

In this letter, electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, and post-mortem X-ray and solution NMR studies were combined to investigate electrolyte degradation phenomena in Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes.
One key finding is that the mechanism for improved performance in EC-free electrolyte arises from improved stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces.

Suppressed solvent oxidation reactions at the cathode, and hence less formation and cross over of acidic species, combined with a graphite SEI more resilient towards acid-mediated decomposition, are shown to be important to improve capacity retention.

Full text excerpt: EC-free electrolytes improve capacity retention via enhanced stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces—suppressing solvent oxidation at the cathode and yielding a graphite SEI more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: battery electrolyte solvent discovery for lithium metal batteries
references: Ritesh Kumar et al. (2025) Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-7nnbl

evidence_type_annotations.claim: principal component analysis

A framework to assess faithfulness of dimensionality reduction methods for preserving chemically meaningful relationships in electrolyte solvent space, enabling data-guided discovery of high-performing non-fluorinated ether electrolytes for lithium metal batteries.
Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections.
Full text excerpt:
A significant reason for this approach is due to the vast, high-dimensional molecular design space for solvents and molecular mixtures.
Here, we present a framework to assess the faithfulness of various dimensionality reduction methods in preserving chemically meaningful relationships.

Then, we apply this framework to guide electrolyte solvent discovery by searching for electrolyte solvents with high cosine similarity to state-of-the-art systems, followed by formulation refinement using domain knowledge.
We then performed experimental validation which revealed different failure modes for different electrolyte selections.

Our findings demonstrate that rational navigation of the electrolyte chemical space, enabled by dimensionality reduction and guided by data, can complement traditional approaches and accelerate the discovery of advanced battery materials.

Full text excerpt: A framework to assess faithfulness of dimensionality reduction methods for preserving chemically meaningful relationships in electrolyte solvent space, enabling data-guided discovery of high-performing non-fluorinated ether electrolytes for lithium metal batteries.
Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections.

references: Jason S. Terreblanche et al. (2024) Electrolyte stability and instability in lithium-ion batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-nmnr8

evidence_type_annotations.claim: electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, post-mortem X-ray, solution NMR

EC-free electrolytes improve capacity retention via enhanced stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces—suppressing solvent oxidation at the cathode and yielding a graphite SEI more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition.

In this letter, electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, and post-mortem X-ray and solution NMR studies were combined to investigate electrolyte degradation phenomena in Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes.

EC-free electrolytes improve capacity retention via enhanced stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces—suppressing solvent oxidation at the cathode and yielding a graphite SEI more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition.

In this letter, electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, and post-mortem X-ray and solution NMR studies were combined to investigate electrolyte degradation phenomena in Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes.
