evidence_type_annotations.claim: review of charge compensation mechanisms
Systematic review linking anionic and cationic redox mechanisms to structural and electronic changes in layered oxide cathodes, and their impact on electrochemical performance.

Depending on the Ni:Co:Mn content, both processes lead to characteristic features in the voltage profile of the cathode materials and performance parameters like the capacity, the cycling stability and the open cell voltage become a function of the composition.
Full text excerpt:

Upon charge and discharge two fundamentally different redox mechanisms are observed: At low and medium states of charge (SOCs) charge compensation takes mainly place at oxygen sites while electron density is shifted from the oxygen lattice to nickel (formation of sigma bonds).

Identification of Ni2+/3+ and Ni3+/4+ redox couples via Ni 2p spectra as a function of voltage; observation of Fermi level shift via Co 2p and O 1s spectra; evidence of non-cationic redox at high voltage (4.8 V) via absence of Ni 2p spectral change despite full Li deintercalation; voltage-dependent...[truncated]

Full text excerpt:
X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) is a promising method to gain information on the electronic structure while inherently limited to a low information depth.

In our previous studies, we developed an in vacuo scratching method, which allowed the removal of a large fraction of the cathode electrolyte interface (CEI) and applied the method to the cathode materials LiCoO2 (LCO) and LiNi0.3Mn0.3Co0.3O2 (NMC333) to investigate the charge state dependent transi...[truncated]

In this study, the in vacuo scratching method was applied to the Ni-rich layered oxide compounds, including LiNi0.6Mn0.2Co0.2O2 (NMC622), LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811) and 2 LiNi0.80Co0.15Al0.05O2 (NCA).
Based on the Co 2p and O 1s photoelectron spectra, a shift to lower binding energies was observed due to the downward shift of the Fermi level.

Li+/Li resulted in fully deintercalated compounds, as shown by the Li 1s photoelectron spectra, while no further change in the Ni 2p photoelectron spectra suggests deviations from the cationic redox process.

Identification of functional-solvent combinations enabling exceptional cycle stability in Ni-rich cathode-based LIBs and elucidation of underlying interfacial stabilization mechanisms.

Abstract: The pervasive use of Ni-rich cathode active materials, e.g., LiNi0.8Mn0.1Co0.1O2 (NMC811), for high energy density Li-ion batteries (LIBs) has been hindered by the rapid capacity decay when cycled at high cutoff voltages, due to electrolyte decomposition of the conventional carbonate solve...[truncated]

These HVE-based batteries demonstrated a significant improvement in cycle stability when operated at a charge cutoff voltage of 4.5 V (vs Li/Li+).
We will show that some unique combinations of the functional solvents could lead to exceptionally stable cycle performance in the Ni-rich cathode-based LIBs.

We will discuss possible fundamental mechanisms responsible for the observed performance stability of the high-voltage LIBs using ex-situ XPS, density functional theory calculations, and other characterization techniques.

references: Jason S. Terreblanche et al. (2024) Electrolyte stability and instability in lithium-ion batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-nmnr8

evidence_type_annotations.claim: electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, post-mortem X-ray, solution NMR

EC-free electrolytes improve capacity retention via enhanced stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces—suppressing solvent oxidation at the cathode and yielding a graphite SEI more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition.

In this letter, electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, and post-mortem X-ray and solution NMR studies were combined to investigate electrolyte degradation phenomena in Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes.
Full text excerpt:

In this letter, electrochemical methods, operando pressure measurements, and post-mortem X-ray and solution NMR studies were combined to investigate electrolyte degradation phenomena in Ni-rich NMC/graphite full cells with EC-containing and EC-free electrolytes.
One key finding is that the mechanism for improved performance in EC-free electrolyte arises from improved stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces.

Suppressed solvent oxidation reactions at the cathode, and hence less formation and cross over of acidic species, combined with a graphite SEI more resilient towards acid-mediated decomposition, are shown to be important to improve capacity retention.

Full text excerpt: EC-free electrolytes improve capacity retention via enhanced stability at both electrode-electrolyte interfaces—suppressing solvent oxidation at the cathode and yielding a graphite SEI more resilient to acid-mediated decomposition.

method_scenario_benchmark_triples.scenario: battery electrolyte solvent discovery for lithium metal batteries
references: Ritesh Kumar et al. (2025) Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-7nnbl

evidence_type_annotations.claim: principal component analysis

A framework to assess faithfulness of dimensionality reduction methods for preserving chemically meaningful relationships in electrolyte solvent space, enabling data-guided discovery of high-performing non-fluorinated ether electrolytes for lithium metal batteries.
Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections.
Full text excerpt:
A significant reason for this approach is due to the vast, high-dimensional molecular design space for solvents and molecular mixtures.
Here, we present a framework to assess the faithfulness of various dimensionality reduction methods in preserving chemically meaningful relationships.

Then, we apply this framework to guide electrolyte solvent discovery by searching for electrolyte solvents with high cosine similarity to state-of-the-art systems, followed by formulation refinement using domain knowledge.
We then performed experimental validation which revealed different failure modes for different electrolyte selections.

Our findings demonstrate that rational navigation of the electrolyte chemical space, enabled by dimensionality reduction and guided by data, can complement traditional approaches and accelerate the discovery of advanced battery materials.

Full text excerpt: A framework to assess faithfulness of dimensionality reduction methods for preserving chemically meaningful relationships in electrolyte solvent space, enabling data-guided discovery of high-performing non-fluorinated ether electrolytes for lithium metal batteries.
Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections.

uniqueness_check.idea: The conflicting claims in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries are retested under matched the experimental, observati...[truncated] If the conflicting claims in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries are retested under matched the experimental, observational, or simulation conditions that differ between the claims conditions, then Coulombic efficiency (CE) will reveal which mechanism is valid and where the disagreement boundary lies: 100 cycles. This proposal addresses the PaperGraph gap 'Potential conclusion conflict: all-solid-state lithium metal batteries contains opposing claims: Li|Li symmetric cell cycling showed very stable and reversible cycling of Li metal over 100 cycles and smooth, dendrite-free deposition morphology. vs Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections..'. It hypothesizes a testable mechanism: Concrete mechanism chain: (1) method capability: X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) with in vacuo scratching contributes through turning a latent physical, chemical, or biological state into a calibrated observable signal; (2) scenario target: in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries, the affected process is a measurable material, molecular, or reaction mechanism under controlled conditions; (3) measurable consequence: the bridge must produce a preregistered change in Coulombic efficiency (CE). The method's input requirements are broad or not clearly specified; the experiment must make them explicit. The required bridge is benchmark dataset, simulation. The decisive prediction is that this concrete bridge, not a generic representation change, will alter Coulombic efficiency (CE); if the bridge data or causal link is absent, the hypothesis should fail rather than be reinterpreted post hoc. MingLi treats this as a structural mutation rather than a surface rephrasing. The study is designed to be falsifiable through Build a minimal benchmark for all-solid-state lithium metal batteries; compare X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) with in vacuo scratching against canonical baselines; measure Coulombic efficiency (CE); include negative controls, ablations, and failure-mode analysis. Grounding evidence comes from: Mingjie Zhang et al. (2024) Advanced High-Voltage Electrolyte Design Using Poly(ethylene oxide) and High-Concentration Ionic Liquids for All-Solid-State Lithium Metal Batteries doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2024-5sqth, Ritesh Kumar et al. (2025) Using electrolyte solvent embeddings to guide battery electrolyte discovery doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-7nnbl. The proposal differs by testing the mapped gap directly with explicit baselines, ablations, and failure-mode criteria.

This proposal addresses the PaperGraph gap 'Potential conclusion conflict: all-solid-state lithium metal batteries contains opposing claims: Li|Li symmetric cell cycling showed very stable and reversible cycling of Li metal over 100 cycles and smooth, dendrite-free deposition morphology. vs Different failure modes observed for different electrolyte selections..'. It hypothesizes a testable mechanism: Concrete mechanism chain: (1) method capability: X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) with in vacuo scratching contributes through turning a latent physical, chemical, or biological state into a calibrated observable signal; (2) scenario target: in all-solid-state lithium metal batteries, the affected process is a measurable material, molecular, or reaction mechanism under controlled conditions; (3) measurable consequence: the bridge must produce a preregistered change in Coulombic efficiency (CE). The method's input requirements are broad or not clearly specified; the experiment must make them explicit. The required bridge is benchmark dataset, simulation. The decisive prediction is that this concrete bridge, not a generic representation change, will alter Coulombic efficiency (CE); if the bridge data or causal link is absent, the hypothesis should fail rather than be reinterpreted post hoc. MingLi treats this as a structural mutation rather than a surface rephrasing. The study is designed to be falsifiable through Build a minimal benchmark for all-solid-state lithium metal batteries; compare X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) with in vacuo scratching against canonical baselines; measure Coulombic efficiency (CE); include negative controls, ablations, and failure-mode analysis. The method's input requirements are broad or not clearly specified; the experiment must make them explicit. The required bridge is benchmark dataset, simulation. The decisive prediction is that this concrete bridge, not a generic representation change, will alter Coulombic efficiency (CE); if the bridge data or causal link is absent, the hypothesis should fail rather than be reinterpreted post hoc. independence.policy: Require distinct model ids or role channels. Qwen-only multi-model setups such as qwen-max/qwen-plus/qwen-deep-research are allowed.

safety_gates.evidence_gate: Debate revisions are adopted only if tied to PaperGraph evidence, YanZhen issue, or an explicit missing-evidence condition.
safety_gates.convergence_gate: If two rounds add no substantive revision, terminate with best current hypothesis and unresolved issues.

noise_level: nominal signal quality -> high-noise or missing-data condition

biological_context: reported cohort/model organism/context -> different cohort, tissue, organism, or stress condition
intervention_dose_or_time: reported dose/time window -> 0.1x/10x dose, duration, or sampling interval
